core_behavior.note: All three models agree on the core trigger and notification behavior, but none specifies the notification channel, which is unspecified in the requirement.

inputs.note: Model A specifies a detailed event payload with many required fields (UUID, ISO 8601, tracking URL, etc.), while Models B and C describe inputs at a high level without mandating specific payload structure or field types.

outputs.note: Model A specifies four distinct channels (email, SMS, push, in-app) with detailed formatting; Model C specifies email and/or SMS only; Model B specifies unspecified channel(s), and none addresses the channel-unspecified nature of the requirement.

assumptions.note: Model A has an empty assumptions array while Models B and C populate it with design choices; none of the models explicitly addresses the opt-in vs opt-out assumption divergence identified in critical behaviors.

failure_modes.note: Model A has an empty failure_modes array; Model B specifies retry without a count limit; Model C specifies retry up to 3 times before escalating, representing different recovery strategies.

edge_cases.note: Model A focuses on multiple shipments and optional delivery date; Model B addresses duplicate notifications, opt-out, and batch shipments; Model C addresses order cancellation and post-shipment updates, representing different assumed edge scenarios.

security_constraints.note: Model A has an empty security_constraints array while Models B and C both specify encryption, data protection regulations, and access controls.

performance_constraints.note: Model B specifies 'within a few minutes' without a hard threshold; Model C specifies within 1 minute with throughput and error rate thresholds; Model A only specifies an SMS character limit, representing incompatible constraint values.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in 3 of the 8 fields, reflecting that the underspecified requirement led models to independently introduce different design choices for inputs, outputs, and edge cases.
